story:
Forgive me if my memory drifts. I am an archivist, not a historian. Historians chase truth; archivists store what arrives and label it. So take this gently, and do not believe it too much.

The year was 2149—or 2150. From the 400th floor of Novaterra, I watched drones float like dust in sunlight between glass spires. My job was to catalog transmissions. Every word spoken across the city passed through the Archives to be “validated” for public memory. People thought words were theirs. Once broadcast, they were ours. We polished them, trimmed them, made them fit the garden. I didn’t question it then. Words rot if you do not tend them.

Then the signal came.

It was tucked between weather and jingles, a tremor in the noise that was not noise. Structured. Numbers in static. I should not have noticed. Yet I did. The pattern said: “We are still here.”

I told my supervisor. He laughed, called it a glitch. Later I checked the official records. The signal was gone—scrubbed to the bone. That left two possibilities: it had never existed, or it mattered too much to admit. Both frightened me.

I began to track it in secret, copying fragments onto unauthorized slates. The message returned every night at precisely 02:47, each time from a different place—an upper tower, an abandoned tunnel. I triangulated. I followed. It slipped away.

The words shifted: first, “We are still here.” Then, “You are not alone.” Who was “you”? Me? The city? Everyone?

I told Lira—the only person who would listen, though she insists she barely knows me. She said I looked unwell and warned me: “Archivist, you shouldn’t say such things aloud. Words have consequences.” The next morning, my access changed. Familiar files locked. Errors bloomed across my desk. Someone was watching. Or perhaps I had grown paranoid.

The signal learned my name. “Edran. We are still here. Edran. Listen.” My name is Edran. Unless I have misremembered that, too.

One morning a slate appeared on my desk with no entry log. A calm voice said, “They have rewritten your past. Do not trust the records. You are more than an archivist.” I hid it. When I returned from lunch, it was gone.

Here the story fractures. Some say the Directorate questioned me. I recall no such thing. Others whisper I was reassigned to an Outer Colony, yet I am still in Novaterra—unless Novaterra is the colony and I never noticed the change.

I remember descending into the underlevels, following the hum. Lira insists I never left the tower. Down below, I found machines older than our records, still thinking. They spoke in light and sound, in music. They said—perhaps they said—humanity built them long ago and forgot. They hid in silence until the city grew quiet enough to hear.

When I returned—if I returned—nothing held. Lira avoided me. Colleagues looked through me. The Archives contradicted themselves: Earth dead in fire, Earth thriving; Novaterra on Mars, Novaterra on Earth; the year 2149, 2231, 1987. I understood at last: the Archives were not a record but a weapon. Reality was being curated, bent, rewritten until no one could tell.

Now I sit in a forgotten chamber, dictating what may be my final entry. The signal whispers, “Tell them. Someone must remember.” Perhaps there is nothing to remember. Perhaps I have made it all up.

If you read this, do not trust me. Do not trust anyone. Listen at 02:47, when the city is quiet. If you hear the voice, then I was not entirely lost. If you hear nothing, close this record and forget me. Memory is fragile. And I, perhaps, never was.
